cue: Say someone has good sense (not book-learning)

es.literal: to have two fingers of forehead
es.meaning: To have common sense; to show basic good judgment. Usually heard in the negative.
es.example: Si tuviera dos dedos de frente, no habría firmado ese contrato.
es.exampleTranslation: If he had any sense, he wouldn't have signed that contract.
es.regionNote: Understood pan-regionally; reviewer to confirm how current it is in Mexico versus plainer 'tener cabeza'. The image is phrenological — forehead height read as intelligence.

it.literal: to have salt in the gourd
it.meaning: To have good sense; to be sensible. Usually heard in the negative.
it.example: Se avesse un po' di sale in zucca, non firmerebbe quel contratto.
it.exampleTranslation: If he had any sense, he wouldn't sign that contract.
it.regionNote: zucca is colloquial for 'head'. Related: 'essere una zucca vuota' — an empty gourd, i.e. a fool.

bridge.note: Both languages measure sense by a physical quantity in the head — and both mostly use it to say someone hasn't got any. Spanish measures the forehead in finger-widths, a leftover of reading intelligence off the skull; Italian measures salt in the gourd, salt being both precious and, since Latin sal, a word for wit. Neither is about knowledge: you can have a doctorate and still, in both languages, be short two fingers or an empty gourd.

interference.es_to_it: 'Due dita di fronte' is not the Italian idiom — the sense lives in the gourd: avere sale in zucca.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener sal en la calabaza' means nothing in Spanish. The measure is the forehead: dos dedos de frente.

review.notes: Banked 2026-07-18 from the candidates queue, prompted by Joe's interest in the knowledge/wisdom distinction. Both sides and the pairing are Claude's; the Latin sal = wit/salt link and the phrenological reading of 'dos dedos de frente' are Claude's etymological claims, unverified — reviewers to confirm or cut. ES reviewer: is this current in MX?
